summary: Gruber challenges the narrative that iPhone XR demand is weak due to poor price-to-feature balance, arguing instead that the issue may be the opposite: the XR offers too much newness, not too little. He notes that annual supplier cut reports are often misleading and that Apple works further ahead than people realize. The consensus among reviewers was that the XR is an excellent value, making the XS harder to justify. Gruber theorizes that some customers are deterred by the unfamiliar X-class experience — no home button, new gestures, Face ID — with no 'familiar' new iPhone option available in 2018. He draws a parallel to the iPhone SE's surprising popularity, partly driven by customers gravitating toward what they already knew. Apple is right to push forward, but may be paying a short-term price for leaving change-averse customers with no comfortable alternative.
key_points: Annual reports of Apple cutting supplier orders are often misleading and shouldn't be taken as reliable indicators of demand
The reviewer consensus was that iPhone XR is a better value than the XS, undermining the 'wrong price/feature balance' narrative
The real issue may be that all 2018 iPhones require adopting the unfamiliar X-class experience, with no 'familiar' option for change-averse buyers
Previous iPhone innovations like Retina displays and Touch ID were additive, not disruptive to existing habits — the X-class experience requires giving things up
Apple is strategically correct to push forward, but may underestimate how many customers resist change even when change is an improvement
money_quotes: You can't judge this from poor-mouthing by Apple's Asian suppliers. Every year, right around now, reports appear that Apple suppliers are claiming Apple has cut orders because demand for the latest iPhones is weaker than Apple projected. Every year.
Maybe the iPhone XR is less in demand not because it offers too little compared to the XS, but rather too much.
There are many people out there who do not like change, period.
A skeptical would-be iPhone X/XS/XR customer, on the other hand, has to willingly give up Touch ID and trust that they'll find Face ID to be an acceptable replacement.
From the objective standpoint of an informed buyer, it's the XS iPhones that look like a worse value proposition, not the XR.
They have minutes, and in just a few minutes, the iPhone XR is not going to feel familiar.

Most companies with a hit product get into long-term trouble by sticking with the tried-and-true that made their product a success in the first place. Not rocking the boat works — until a disruptive competitor rocks your boat for you.
Some people are resistant to change even when change is called for.
key_insight: The iPhone XR's potential demand weakness may stem not from offering too little for its price, but from Apple eliminating the familiar iPhone experience entirely, leaving change-averse customers with no comfortable upgrade path.
